name: Marty Grosz
abstract: Martin Oliver Grosz (born February 28, 1930) is a German-born American jazz guitarist, banjoist, vocalist, and composer born in Berlin, Germany, the son of artist George Grosz. He performed with Bob Wilber and wrote arrangements for him.

interviews.title: Marty Grosz interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 4, 1995

concerts.label: New York Jazz Repertory Company: The Music of Jelly Roll Morton
Newport Jazz Festival: The Stardust Road: A Hoagy Carmichael Jubilee
Kool Jazz Festival: Goin' to Chicago

quotes.text: Yeah. I’ve got an old-time guitar player friend who called me up not long ago, and his son is playing lead guitar for Rod Stewart in Europe for a year, and he makes, this guy I suppose in his career if he made three or four hundred dollars a week it was a good week. His son makes five thousand dollars a week, plus a hundred and forty dollars a day for food you know and stuff, and he told me, he described the column speakers they had and everything. And he said that the over-thirty guys who go to listen to this music, the audiences, if they don’t feel a sort of sound system that actually gives them a kind of constriction of the chest, then they want their money back. And he says the guys in the band, there’s no monitors anymore, they all use, I didn’t know this, they all use earphones because they’re all getting deaf, hearing loss — and I’m wondering to myself, I mean that’s about as far removed from anything that I’ve ever gotten into. The closest I get are these deaf soundmen you have to work with. You know these cretins. Can you imagine Carnegie Hall? They’ve got a sound man, now this was twenty years ago, George Wein was doing a bunch of concerts and I was standing, waiting in the back and there was a sound man and he’s got one of these work shirts on, and it says on the back “Carnegie Hall” and on the front it says his name, you know “Al” and he’s pushing, he’s working the sound board. And they’re doing a band up there, and the guy is playing an alto sax solo, and he’s pushing the gain on the trombone. And I said, “You know that’s an alto sax that’s playing, not a trombone.” “Oh, yeah, oh yeah, thanks a lot, there.” I mean the artists’ work is in the hands of these cretins, or creetins, depends whether you’re in England, in England they call them creetins. I mean this is like getting the Boston Symphony to record on one of those things you used to have in those booths you’d go in, you know, and send a message home to mom [holds nose] “Hi Mom.”
quotes.caption: Anecdote about a deaf soundman at a George Wein concert
quotes.doc_title: Marty Grosz interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 4, 1995
